10. NVS from Sparse Views and Auto Encoders

What if we have a scene with not so many camera images? Then we can’t just overfit a scene. What if we want to represent new scenes of new objects (OOD)?

What priors can we learn from observations only?

10.1. Autoencoders

What about self-supervised scene representations? Try an auto-encoder like model where the decoder is conditioned on output pose (by ihjecting into bottleneck):

We get alright results but it doesn’t seem to have good understanding of 3D structure. How do we incorporate that?

Introduce differentiable 3D to 2D renderer as the decoder so that some of the underlying representation uses 3D. Use previous representations and renderers

For example, the encoder can predict the parameters of the scene representation. For example, latent code can be mapped to weights and biases of neural field via a hypernetwork

Other things we can do include predicting parameters of voxel grid, and concatenating latent code to query coordinate as input of a nerf. One example that sitzmann worked on is a scene representation network where the latent is used as the input to a hypernetwork that produces a SDF network based on SIREN

General setup: The encoder predicts understands what the object is, the decoder creates the scene and then we render it.

10.2. Techniques

10.2.1. Inference-time optimization of the latent

How do we embed even more 3D inductive bias, this time into the encoder?

Instead of doing an encoder, you can freeze the decoder and find the best that reconstructs the output. And then we can render from any other camera pose.

10.2.2. [TODO] PixelNerf

Suppose we have one input image. When ray marching from some other angle, project each 3D point onto input view. Take those image features and feed that as well as the 3D location into the MLP. This generalizes across scenes better. That makes the prediction problem closer to “given what this point looks like in the input, infer its 3D properties“.

10.2.3. Light Field networks

We can even remove 3D bias by using Plucker coordiantes that takes in the latent and the field MLP. This is less egometrically crisp and requires more training data.

10.3. Does this shit matter

The Parable of the Parser (a lesson from NLP): Generations of researchers built sophisticated syntactic parsers — noun phrases, verb phrases, dependency trees. Today, LLMs render this entire subfield irrelevant because they solve downstream tasks without ever building a parse tree. The lesson: some intermediate problems we thought were fundamental turn out to be fake — they were only necessary because we lacked compute and data, not because the real task required them.
